exemplar.title: How does changing the time in 5 minute intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 minutes) for which 3 grams of Phaseolus vulgaris (Lightly speckled kidney beans) are simmered affect its iron content in milligrams as determined by spectrophotometric methods using a wavelength of 477.1 nm?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does changing the time in 5 minute intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 minutes) for which 3 grams of Phaseolus vulgaris (Lightly speckled kidney beans) are simmered affect its iron content in milligrams as determined by spectrophotometric methods using a wavelength of 477.1 nm?

strengths: The introduction establishes real-world context and purpose clearly, linking dietary iron to kidney-bean preparation.
The research question is stated verbatim, specifies quantitative variables, and remains coherent throughout.
The methodology is comprehensive: detailed materials list, variable table, calibration curve procedure and uncertainty treatment.
Control of variables and a thorough risk assessment demonstrate careful planning.
The title and repeated restatement of the research question reinforce focus and coherence.
weaknesses: Minor theoretical digressions on d-orbital splitting slightly blur the overall focus.
The combustion stage diverges from the initial spectrophotometric plan and is not fully aligned with the research question.
Sample-mass assumptions (eight beans = 3 g) introduce potential variability.
The subjective end-point for combustion could reduce reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in the 5â6 band for focus and method: topic communication is accurate and effective; the research question is clearly stated and well connected; the methodology is thorough with clear evidence of informed selection. Minor digressions and methodological mismatches prevent a top-band (6) award, making 5 the appropriate holistic score.

strengths: Background on the ironâthiocyanate complex is effectively integrated from source material.
Key concepts (BeerâLambert law, solution preparation, propagated uncertainty) are explained clearly and linked to the investigation.
Technical terminology (molar extinction coefficient, AU, ppm, Pearsonâs r) is used accurately and consistently.
Calculations from USDA data provide good external validation of results.
weaknesses: Diagrams for ligand-field theory are overcomplicated and contain structural errors.
Some web citations lack full authorâyear details, reducing traceability.
Assuming 100 % extraction of iron from beans is not justified or discussed.
Occasional typographical and citation inconsistencies detract from coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall performance fits the top of the 3â4 band: source material application is mostly relevant; subject knowledge is clear but contains minor conceptual gaps; terminology is consistently accurate. Errors in orbital diagrams and incomplete citations prevent a higher band (5â6), so a score of 4 reflects the demonstrated understanding and appropriate use of sources.

strengths: Calibrated curve with error bars is well constructed and supports reliable concentration determination.
Uncertainty propagation is applied correctly, and each step is documented transparently.
Percentage-error calculations effectively communicate low systematic bias.
Evaluation quantifies bean-mass variability and proposes procedural improvements.
weaknesses: Statistical treatment remains elementary (no significance testing or outlier analysis).
Subjective criterion for combustion end-point reduces repeatability.
Sample-mass variability due to assuming eight beans = 3 g is not addressed experimentally.
Uncertainty in the calibration-curve slope is not incorporated into propagated error.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments indicate 'good' but not 'excellent' critical thinking: research is mostly relevant (5), analysis is clear but only partly supported (4), and evaluation is competent without deep critical sophistication (4). Averaging these strands and considering the balance of strengths and weaknesses justifies a holistic score of 4 out of 12.

strengths: The report follows the required Extended Essay structure with logical headings and properly labeled figures/tables.
Formatting is consistent: page numbers, word count, fonts, and referencing style meet conventions.
Tables of contents and figure captions aid navigation and understanding.
weaknesses: No abstract is included, deviating from standard EE conventions.
A paragraph is truncated mid-sentence, indicating minor proofreading oversights.
Materials list is exhaustive but could be better organized into logical categories.
Lengthy theoretical sections slightly hinder readability and flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets most conventions (structure, layout, labeling) and flows logically, placing it in the 3â4 band. Minor omissions (missing abstract, truncated text) and readability issues prevent the top score of 4, so a score of 3 accurately reflects the presentation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows the required EE structure (title page, abstract omitted per new guide, contents, numbered sections, references).  Headings are logical, tables/figures labeled, and the flow is easy to follow.  Minor issues such as excessively long theoretical sub-sections and occasional duplication of information slightly hinder overall coherence, so 3 of 4 is appropriate.

The introduction clearly establishes the real-world context of dietary iron and explicitly links this to kidney-bean preparation. Definitions, chemical principles (complex formation, Beer-Lambert) and the overall purpose are articulated in language that is consistently accurate.  The reader immediately understands what is being investigated and why, showing clear focus and purpose.  Minor digressions (e.g., very long theoretical digressions on d-orbital splitting) slightly blur the focus, so the strand sits solidly in the 5â6 band but not at its very top.

Data are processed to concentrations, converted to mg iron, graphed with uncertainties, and Pearsonâs r and rÂ² are calculated.  The analysis identifies random error and compares measured values to USDA data.  However, statistical treatment is elementary (no significance testing) and the linear fit is applied despite obvious scatter.  Conclusions are only partly supported, so analysis is âclearâ but not âwell-supportedâ, meriting the lower half of the 4â6 band.

Five trials across six time points yield a reasonable data set and calibration curve; the work remains mostly relevant to the research question.  Weaknesses such as very small sample mass and subjective combustion criterion reduce overall research strength, placing it in the good (4â6) rather than excellent range; a mid-band score of 5 is justified.

The student explains spectrophotometry, BeerâLambert law and iron nutrition accurately and links these to the investigation.  Calculations for solution preparation and propagated uncertainty show competent chemical understanding.  Nonetheless, the rationale for converting absorbance to mg iron assumes 100 % extraction with limited justification, and the orbital discussion contains structural errors, so knowledge is clear but not fully coherentâconsistent with a 4.

Throughout the essay, terms such as "molar extinction coefficient", "propagated uncertainty", "pearsonâs correlation coefficient" and correct units (ppm, AU) are used accurately and consistently.  Occasional typographical slips do not impede clarity.  This sustained, correct use of chemistry terminology aligns with the top band, though not flawless enough to justify the full 6.

The essay presents a full materials list, detailed variable table, calibration-curve preparation, step-by-step procedure (including repeats) and uncertainty treatment.  Source selection (literature on iron complexes and food composition) appears purposeful.  Limitations such as bean-mass variability are recognised.  Method is coherent yet some elements (subjective end-point for combustion, assumption of equal bean mass, modest sample size) signal that selection of methods is not entirely optimal, warranting 5 rather than 6.

Page numbers, word count, clear tables, figure captions and consistent font are evident.  Graphs include error bars and axes with units.  Referencing style is consistent.  Layout fully meets EE conventions with no significant lapses, justifying the top score of 4 within this band.

The student discusses random vs systematic error, proposes procedural improvements, and evaluates reliability through standard deviation and percentage error.  The evaluation is thoughtful yet not deeply critical; it does not, for example, quantify how improvements would influence uncertainty or fully explore alternative methodologies.  This aligns with a competent but not highly sophisticated discussion, fitting score 4.

Scientific papers, data-booklet values and safety sheets are cited and integrated into background and evaluation.  Source material underpins theory and real-world context; however, when discussing ligand field theory the diagrams become muddled and citations are sometimes generic (e.g., open-ended web pages).  The application is therefore mostly relevant but not consistently strong, fitting the 3â4 band at the top end.

The research question is stated verbatim in the title and again in section 2, specifying quantitative variables (3 g beans, 0â25 min, spectrophotometric iron content at 477.1 nm).  This wording is sufficiently narrow to be investigated within an EE and is carried forward throughout method, data, analysis and conclusion.  A slight weakness is that the question does not acknowledge the combustion stage that is later introduced, suggesting partial mismatch between planning and execution; therefore a mid-high score of 5 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The preliminary trials description introduces the LGV approach, but the rationale for increasing leaf area remains unclearâcould you clarify that choice?
Introducing the combustion stage diverges from the initial spectrophotometric focus; discuss how this aligns with your original methodology plan.
Table 1 clearly enumerates control variables with their impact and control methods, demonstrating careful methodological planning.
The materials list is exhaustive but could be grouped into logical categories (e.g., glassware, chemicals, instrumentation) to improve readability.
The CâVâ calculation for preparing calibration standards is accurate and clearly presented.
The title succinctly states the investigationâs variables and purpose, immediately orienting the reader to the focus of the study.
The research question is clearly embedded in the opening paragraph and specifies the independent and dependent variables precisely.
The word count falls within the 4000-word limit and is clearly indicated, satisfying formatting requirements.
The table of contents is comprehensive, but the absence of an abstract section deviates from standard EE conventionsâconsider adding it.
The citation (âIronâ) lacks author and year details, reducing source traceability; ensure each web source has full bibliographic information.
The third paragraph is truncated midâsentence (âIron-richâ); verify that the text block fully captures your intended content.
The background on ironâthiocyanate complex formation integrates source material effectively to underpin the spectrophotometric method.
Restating the research question in section 2 reinforces the focus and demonstrates coherence between title and investigation.
The LaTeX code for the repeated d-orbital matrices is malformed and overcomplicated; simplify the orbital splitting diagram and correct syntax.
Figure 1âs caption clearly credits student creation via Canva, but including the date of creation would strengthen reproducibility.
The color-wheel figure is well labelled and its caption contextualizes data from the IB data booklet effectively.
Background dilution calculations correctly apply $C_1V_1=C_2V_2$. This shows strong command of solution preparation principles.
When converting 0.1 g Lâ»Â¹ to moles, a rounding protocol should be stated explicitly to avoid propagation of rounding error.
Figure 4 shows a well-constructed calibration curve with error bars, supporting reliable concentration determination.
Assuming eight beans equals 3 g introduces significant random error due to beanâtoâbean mass variation; weigh each sample to improve precision.
Determining combustion completion by color alone is subjective; using a fixed heating time or mass plateau would standardize ash recovery.
The risk assessment table comprehensively covers chemical, physical and environmental hazards with clear mitigation methods.
Raw absorbance data exhibit large scatter; consider statistical outlier tests or increasing replicates to improve reliability.
Processed ironâcontent table highlights standard deviations exceeding propagated uncertainty, indicating imprecision that warrants further discussion.
Concentration calculation from the calibration line is correct, but the uncertainty in the slope should be incorporated when propagating error.
The uncertainty propagation formula is applied correctly and clearly documents each step of the calculation.
Figure 7 effectively visualizes iron content versus cooking time with error bars, aiding interpretation of trends and uncertainties.
The theoretical iron content calculation from USDA data aligns closely with your 0-minute control result, demonstrating good external validation.
The percentage error calculation is accurate and clearly communicates the low systematic bias of the method.
Quantifying bean-mass variability with standard deviation in the evaluation demonstrates analytical reflection on a key random error source.
Works cited list is thorough, but some online resources lack consistent access dates; standardize citation format for all entries.
